nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still floodplain waters, flooded forest (igapó and várzea), lake margins, and sheltered river backwaters of the central and western Amazon Basin; typically associated with submerged roots, woody debris, and leaf litter in soft, blackwater or clearwater environments

decorPreferences: large pieces of driftwood (roots, branches) as shelter and territory markers
subdued lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) or tall background plants (Amazon swords, vallisneria)
dark substrate (fine dark sand or fine gravel) to reduce stress and enhance coloration
leaf litter (Indian almond / Catappa leaves) to lower pH and release tannins
minimal flow — gentle circulation only; avoid strong currents
open mid-water swimming space central to the tank

diet.notes: Wild discus are primarily carnivorous, feeding on invertebrates, insect larvae, and small crustaceans. In the aquarium, high-protein foods are essential: beefheart-based frozen/fresh mixes (traditional staple), frozen bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, high-quality discus-specific pellets and granules. Many experienced keepers use a beefheart mix as the dietary foundation. Live foods (earthworms, blackworms) accepted and beneficial for conditioning breeders. Feed 2–3 small portions daily; remove uneaten food promptly to protect water quality. Farm-raised specimens usually adapt more readily to prepared foods than wild-caught.

goodWith: other discus (conspecifics — kept in groups of 6+)
cardinal tetras (Paracheirodon axelrodi) — classic biotope companion
corydoras catfish (C. sterbai preferred — higher temperature tolerance)
German blue rams (Mikrogeophagus ramirezi — compatible temperature)
peaceful South American tetras tolerant of warm soft water
avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous fish that will stress discus
fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, some serpae tetras)
common plecos (disruptive, outcompete for food at night)
small shrimp during spawning (parents become very territorial)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo for Symphysodon aequifasciatus. Verify availability and licensing before use.
Symphysodon aequifasciatus (Brown Discus color form). Good lateral view showing characteristic disc shape and vertical banding. Photographer: Citron. Recommended candidate.

summary: The Common Discus is one of the most iconic freshwater aquarium fish, prized for its laterally compressed disc shape and vivid coloration spanning brown, blue, and green forms. A demanding species requiring warm, soft, acidic water and excellent water quality; rewarding for advanced keepers.
careNotes:
The Common Discus (Symphysodon aequifasciatus) encompasses the brown, blue, and green wild color forms described before the 2006 Schultz revision that formally split the genus into three species: S. aequifasciatus (this species), S. discus (Heckel Discus), and S. tarzoo (Red Discus). The vast majority of discus in the hobby are captive-bred strains derived primarily from S. aequifasciatus, available in dozens of selectively developed color morphs.

Water quality is the defining challenge of discus keeping. Discus are extremely sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and elevated nitrate; nitrate should ideally be kept below 20 ppm and preferably under 10 ppm. Frequent partial water changes (30–50% twice weekly or daily in breeding setups) are essential. Wild fish demand very soft, acidic, warm blackwater (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–4, 29–31°C), but most farm-raised stock have been acclimated to slightly higher pH (6.8–7.0) and marginally cooler temperatures (27–29°C) for ease of commercial production — this divergence is important when sourcing fish; confirm farm parameters and acclimate gradually. Temperature must remain stable and warm; values below 27°C stress discus and render them susceptible to hole-in-the-head disease (HITH) and Hexamita infections.

Groups of at least 6 are recommended to distribute aggression and allow pair formation. Tank footprint matters more than volume — a 120 × 50 cm base minimum. Discus are slow, deliberate feeders; do not keep them with fast or boisterous species that will outcompete them at feeding time. Cardinal tetras at similar temperature requirements are the classic companion. Corydoras sterbai is the preferred bottom companion as it tolerates the higher temperatures discus require.

Farm-raised vs. wild-caught husbandry differences: Wild-caught specimens require strict blackwater conditions (pH 5.5–6.5, very soft water, tannin-rich environment) and are significantly harder to maintain and breed. Farm-raised fish are generally hardier and more forgiving of slightly less extreme parameters, making them the recommended choice for most hobbyists.
breedingNotes:
Discus are biparental substrate spawners with a uniquely evolved parental care strategy: both parents secrete a nutritious skin mucus ('discus milk') that newly hatched fry exclusively feed on for the first 2–3 weeks of life, after which they can be transitioned to micro-foods (newly hatched brine shrimp, micro-worms). This obligate parental mucus feeding is extremely rare among fishes and is the defining reproductive trait of the genus Symphysodon.

Breeding requires soft, acidic, very warm water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–4, 30–31°C) and a broad flat spawning surface — a clay pot, large flat leaf (Amazon sword leaf), or vertical spawning cone. Pairs form from a group and must be confirmed before isolation; forced pairing is rarely productive. Eggs are typically deposited in clutches of 100–350; both parents fan and guard eggs over a 48–60 hour incubation. Wrigglers are moved to a pit by parents; free-swimming fry immediately begin feeding on parental mucus secretions and should not be separated from parents for the first 10–14 days minimum.

First-time pairs often eat eggs; repeated spawning attempts usually yield improved success. Dedicated breeding tanks (bare bottom, 200+ L, bare or minimal decor) with daily water changes of 20–30% using soft, conditioned water are recommended. Overall breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to strict water parameter requirements, the fry's obligate dependence on parental mucus, and the need for established compatible pairs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Symphysodon aequifasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (28–31°C), pH (5.5–7.0), hardness, native range (Amazon basin floodplains), biotope, feeding notes, breeding behaviour including parental mucus feeding, taxonomy per 2006 Schultz revision.

additional.name: FishBase — Symphysodon aequifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20 cm TL), temperature range, native range (central Amazon, Ucayali–Solimões drainages), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet classification. Confirmed three-species taxonomy following Schultz (2004/2006).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Discus Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Provided tank size guidance (55+ gal / ~208 L for a small group, larger for breeding), temperature 82–88°F (28–31°C), pH 6.0–7.0, feeding recommendations (beefheart mix, frozen bloodworms, discus pellets), careLevel advanced, companion species (cardinal tetras, corydoras sterbai). Minimum 280 L chosen to accommodate a group of 6 adults.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Discus species profile
additional.notes: UK hobby publication. Confirmed soft acidic water requirements, farm-raised vs wild-caught parameter divergence, parental mucus fry feeding biology, group size recommendation (6+), and HITH disease susceptibility below 27°C.

additional.name: Schultz, N. (2004) — Four new species of discus (Symphysodon) with a redescription of Symphysodon discus Heckel
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed taxonomic revision establishing the three currently accepted Symphysodon species: S. aequifasciatus (Common/Blue/Brown/Green Discus), S. discus (Heckel Discus), S. tarzoo (Red/Tefe Discus). Published in Zootaxa 490. This is the authority for the current three-species classification referenced in the care notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner; fry depend exclusively on parental skin-mucus ('discus milk') secretions for 2–3 weeks post-hatching — obligate parental contact required. Clutch 100–350 eggs; incubation ~50 hours; fry free-swimming after ~3 days. Strict soft acidic warm water (pH 5.5–6.5, 30–31°C) required. Pairs form naturally from groups; first spawns frequently fail. Bare-bottom 200+ L dedicated breeding tank with daily large water changes strongly recommended.
